content.assessment: Grade-splitting shares structural logic with Finnish consolidation of upper grades for economies of scale and specialist access, but the Finnish evidence base is specifically about full consolidation, not partial cycle-splitting, so transferability is uncertain. The critical Finnish finding on depopulation acceleration (F-live-finnish_reform-05) applies most directly to full closures; T2's retention of lower grades locally may partially mitigate this by preserving some community anchor function, though this is not tested in the Finnish literature. The consolidation-shock evidence (F-live-finnish_reform-06) is relevant to the upper-grade transition component specifically, since it isolates disruption from consolidation itself independent of school quality — a direct analog to what displaced upper-grade pupils would experience. The segregation risk from church-sector absorption of upper-grade pupils (F-live-finnish_reform-09) echoes Finland's contested but real associations between choice/decentralization and rising segregation (F-live-finnish_reform-07).
opportunities: Could be designed with lessons from Finland's phased, piloted 1972-1977 approach — testing rural viability before wide rollout rather than assuming urban-style consolidation logic transfers directly (F-live-finnish_reform-01)
strengths: Partial retention of local schooling for younger children may reduce exposure to the demographic-acceleration effect documented for full closures, though this is an inference not directly tested (F-live-finnish_reform-05)
Aligns with the logic of Finland's comprehensive-provision approach in aiming to preserve some form of local access rather than eliminating it entirely (F-live-finnish_reform-01)
threats: If upper-grade consolidation follows the same fiscal-rationale logic as Finnish closures, and Finnish follow-up studies rarely verified claimed savings, similar unverified savings claims could underlie site-selection decisions here (F-live-finnish_reform-04)

Partial retention does not eliminate exposure to the demographic-acceleration dynamic if the upper-grade departure still triggers community disinvestment signals, an effect untested for partial models (F-live-finnish_reform-05)
weaknesses: The upper-grade consolidation shock is likely to reproduce the pure consolidation disruption effect documented in Finland's 1999-2000 study, since it isolates disruption from consolidation mechanics regardless of the partial nature of the split (F-live-finnish_reform-06)

Church-sector selective absorption of upper-grade pupils could amplify segregation in a manner structurally similar to Finnish choice-driven sorting, even though causal weighting remains contested in the Finnish case (F-live-finnish_reform-09, F-live-finnish_reform-07)
